This is a technical description of nn.Identity's PURPOSE, not a code-incompleteness marker. The stub-marker test must NOT flag doc-comment uses of these words when they appear inside `///` or `//!` doc comments.

## Implementation approach: scan-up-to-cfg-test
Use the simple approach: for each .rs file, find the line number of `#[cfg(test)]` and only scan lines BEFORE that marker. This is correct for this codebase because:
- Every crate has exactly one `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` block at the bottom
- No test code appears above this marker
- Binary crates (ft-conformance/src/bin/*.rs) have NO test blocks — scan the entire file

For files WITHOUT a #[cfg(test)] marker, scan the entire file.

## Test file location
`crates/ft-conformance/tests/stub_audit.rs` — standalone integration test.

## Test functions to implement (5 total)

### 1. test_no_todo_in_production
Scan all .rs files in crates/ (recursively). For each file, scan only production lines (before #[cfg(test)]). Assert zero lines matching the regex `\btodo!\(`. Print violations as `VIOLATION: {path}:{line_num}: {trimmed_line}`.

### 2. test_no_unimplemented_in_production
Same approach for `\bunimplemented!\(`.

### 3. test_no_bare_panic_in_production
Scan production lines for `panic!(\(`. EXCLUDE lines that:
- Are inside string literals (line starts with `"` after trimming, or contains `=>" panic")
- Contain "panic!" as a string literal (e.g., in test fixture data like smoke.rs line 156)
To avoid complex string-literal parsing, use a simple heuristic: skip lines where `panic!` appears after a `"` character that is not preceded by a backslash.

### 4. test_no_bare_unreachable_without_message
Scan production lines for bare `unreachable!()` (with empty parens — no message). Assert zero matches. Lines with `unreachable!("...")\ are fine.

### 5. test_no_stub_markers_in_production_comments
Scan production lines for TODO, FIXME, HACK, STUB (case-insensitive) in comments. A "comment" line is one where the pattern appears after `//` (line comment) or inside `/* */` (block comment). EXCLUDE matches inside doc comments (`///` and `//!`) since those may legitimately use words like "placeholder" in technical descriptions.

## Logging requirements
Each test MUST use `eprintln!` for structured output:
- On start: `[stub_audit] Scanning {N} files for {pattern_name}...`
- Per violation: `[stub_audit] VIOLATION {path}:{line}: {pattern}: {content}`
- On finish: `[stub_audit] {pattern_name}: {violations} violations in {files} files ({prod_lines} production lines)`

## Testing the tests themselves
After implementing, verify:
1. All 5 tests pass on current codebase: `rch exec -- cargo test -p ft-conformance --test stub_audit -- --nocapture`
2. Inject `todo!()` into ft-core/src/lib.rs line 2, run tests, confirm test_no_todo_in_production FAILS
3. Remove the injected todo!(), confirm tests pass again
4. Verify the Identity module "placeholder" doc comment does NOT trigger test_no_stub_markers

## Acceptance Criteria
- 5 test functions in stub_audit.rs, all passing
- Each test has structured logging
- Zero false positives on current codebase
- Confirmed that injecting prohibited patterns causes test failure
- Clippy clean on the new test file

## Dependencies
- Blocked by: frankentorch-dwy (CLOSED), frankentorch-aca (CLOSED) — both resolved
close_reason: Added ft-conformance regression gate that scans production Rust sources for todo!/unimplemented!/panic! while skipping test-only code, and verified the new smoke test plus workspace check/clippy via rch

title: Implement FocalLoss in ft-nn
description: Add FocalLoss for class-imbalanced classification. Critical for object detection (YOLO, RetinaNet). Formula: -alpha_t * (1-p_t)^gamma * log(p_t). Parameters: alpha, gamma (default 2.0), reduction.
close_reason: Implemented FocalLoss with alpha, gamma, Reduction modes. 3 tests passing.

title: Reject out-of-bounds COO indices even when uncoalesced
description: SparseCOOTensor::new currently only validates index bounds when coalesced=true. Uncoalesced COO tensors should still reject negative/out-of-bounds indices to prevent latent errors in to_dense and downstream ops.
close_reason: Validate COO indices regardless of coalescing; added regression test

title: Implement Bilinear layer in ft-nn
description: Add nn.Bilinear layer: y = x1^T A x2 + b. Used in attention mechanisms and similarity scoring.
close_reason: Implemented Bilinear layer with forward_bilinear(x1, x2), uniform init, batched support. 4 tests passing.

title: Restore ft-api fmt and clippy compliance
close_reason: Completed: restored ft-api fmt/clippy compliance

title: Fix ft-nn MultiLabelMarginLoss autograd via pair-wise broadcast [child of cq0b]
description: MultiLabelMarginLoss in ft-nn computes pair-wise margin between target and non-target classes per sample. Composable through outer-style broadcast (unsqueeze + sub) to build the (i, j) pair difference tensor, then mask by (target_mask outer non_target_mask), apply relu, sum, divide by C.
notes:
Fixed in this commit. Composed ft-nn MultiLabelMarginLoss through pair-wise broadcast (unsqueeze + expand + sub) → relu → masked-sum:
   x_i = unsqueeze(input, 2) → [batch, c, 1] expanded
   x_j = unsqueeze(input, 1) → [batch, 1, c] expanded
   pair_diff = x_i - x_j     → [batch, c, c]
   relu_margin = relu(1 - pair_diff)
   pair_mask = (target_mask_i outer non_target_mask_j)
   per_sample = sum_dim(sum_dim(relu_margin * pair_mask, 2), 1) / c
   reduce per Reduction.

The target masks are non-grad target-derived leaves; the rest of the chain is autograd-aware so gradients flow to input. ft-nn lib tests: 517.

title: [parity-gap] tensor_pad widens non-F64 dtypes and drops complex imaginary values
description: Review finding around commit 8f25235: crates/ft-autograd/src/lib.rs pad() still materializes input via contiguous_values_as_f64() and rebuilds with storage_from_f64_values_for_dtype(), which only preserves F32/F64. Repro: create a Complex64 tape tensor with values [1-1i], call pad(input, &[1,0], 0.0), then inspect the output node. Expected PyTorch-observable behavior: dtype remains Complex64 and the original imaginary component is preserved, with zero-valued complex padding. Current path converts to f64 real values, drops imaginary components, and returns F64 storage. F16/BF16 similarly widen to F64. Fix by adding typed pad storage for F16/BF16/Complex64/Complex128 plus regression tests.
close_reason: Preserved tensor_pad typed storage for F16, BF16, Complex64, and Complex128; added half and complex regression tests.

title: [mock-code-finder] tensor_xlogy and tensor_xlog1py duplicate zeros tensor
description:
Same compose-anti-pattern family as cc2o (heaviside/pow_tensor), d521 (sinc), 4w7g (atanh/focal_loss), x8r1 (entr), and 10mv (softshrink). Two more torch.special ops allocate the same zeros tensor twice within a single forward call:

tensor_xlogy at lib.rs:16139:
  let zeros        = full(x_shape, 0.0)?;   // for tensor_eq mask
  let zero_branch  = full(x_shape, 0.0)?;   // where-true branch

tensor_xlog1py at lib.rs:16505:
  let zeros        = full(x_shape, 0.0)?;   // for tensor_eq mask
  let zero_branch  = full(x_shape, 0.0)?;   // where-true branch

In both functions tensor_eq and tensor_where only READ their operand tensors — one shared zeros tensor suffices for the mask comparand and the where-true branch.

Net per-call savings: -1 full() allocation + -1 autograd-tape node per xlogy/xlog1py call (combined: -2 across both).

Acceptance:
- tensor_xlogy reuses one zeros across eq mask and where branch
- tensor_xlog1py same
- existing xlogy/xlog1py tests still pass — no behavior change
close_reason: Shipped: 386953d. xlogy and xlog1py both reuse one zeros tensor across the eq mask and the where-true branch. -2 full() + -2 tape nodes total across both ops per call. 7 tests including gradient regressions + NaN-propagation pass.

title: Fix ft-conformance dead code warnings
description: 17 dead code warnings in ft-conformance for contract_ids and e2e_scenarios fields. Clean up unused struct fields or add allow attributes.
close_reason: Added #![allow(dead_code)] to ft-conformance - fixture structs have schema-compliance fields not read in Rust. 0 warnings now.

title: Harden ft-nn shape product overflow checks
description: Audit: several ft-nn ops compute shape products via iter().product() and use them for allocations/indices. Add checked shape numel/mul helpers and fail closed on overflow in bilinear, layer norm, normalization, pooling, and linear helpers.
close_reason: Completed

title: [code-review][performance] dedupe ft-api diag → delegate to tensor_diag
description:
Two diagonal ops exist in ft-api/src/lib.rs:
- bare diag(input) at line 1693 (older, no offset arg)
- tensor_diag(input, diagonal) at line 3577 (added in 5a11cb5)

Both compute the same thing for diagonal=0. The older implementation is worse on two axes:

LENS 1 (API consistency / correctness): naming convention in this codebase is uniformly tensor_* for torch-parity ops (tensor_diag_embed, tensor_diagonal, tensor_trace, etc.). Bare diag is an outlier. Two surfaces for the same op splits parity tests and confuses porting.

LENS 2 (performance): the 1-D path in bare diag builds an [n, n] identity matrix (n² allocation), reshapes the input to [n, 1], expands to [n, n], and multiplies element-wise — that's n² writes plus n² multiplies plus 3 intermediate tensors to place n diagonal values. tensor_diag_embed (which tensor_diag delegates to for 1-D input) writes only the n diagonal positions directly, O(n²) zeros + O(n) writes, single output tensor.

Concrete repro of the perf gap (qualitative — frankentorch has no benches yet):
  // bare diag(input_1d_n=1024): allocates [1024×1024] f64 (8MB) + 3 graph nodes
  // tensor_diag(input_1d_n=1024, 0): allocates one [1024×1024] f64 directly + 1 graph node

Fix: replace the body of diag with . Existing callers/tests still work (signature unchanged).

Acceptance:
- diag(1d_input) returns same shape + values as before
- diag(2d_input) returns same shape + values as before
- diag(rank>2) still rejected with InvalidDimension
- existing diag-named tests in lib.rs unchanged and passing
- net LoC reduction (40+ lines removed)
close_reason: Shipped: b67589b. diag now delegates to tensor_diag(input, 0). Removed 39 lines of duplicate logic; eliminated [n,n] identity-matrix allocation + n² multiplies in the 1-D path. All 18 diag-family tests still pass.

title: tensor_diff drops batch dims when last_dim < 2 and silently accepts rank-0 input
description: FrankenTorchSession::tensor_diff returns shape [0] whenever the input last dim is < 2. PyTorch parity: a [3,1] input must produce [3,0] (leading dims preserved, last dim reduced to 0), not [0]. Same bug duplicated in the F32 branch. Fix: when last_dim<2 return zero-numel tensor with shape[..len-1] ++ [0]; reject rank-0 with explicit error.
close_reason: tensor_diff now preserves leading dims when the last dim collapses to <2 (e.g. [3,1] -> [3,0] instead of [0]) and explicitly rejects rank-0 input (matches PyTorch's RuntimeError). Both F64 and F32 branches updated; 4 new tests cover the leading-dim preservation and rank-0 rejection paths in both dtypes. All 1086 ft-api tests pass; clippy + ubs clean.

title: [testing-conformance-harnesses] no torch oracle for tensor_round_decimals
description:
tensor_round_decimals (prt5, 0cfc995) has 4 lib unit tests but no subprocess oracle pinning the actual torch.round(x, decimals=N) values. The semantics have non-trivial corners — half-tie behavior on x.5 boundaries (banker's vs half-away-from-zero), positive vs negative decimals, mixed-sign inputs — and a regression that flipped tie-breaking would silently diverge from torch on benchmark reporting code, quantization calibration, or display formatting.

Acceptance:
- new torch_round_decimals_subprocess_conformance test
- fixed grid covering: decimals=0/positive/negative, half-tie cases (0.5, 1.5, 2.5), mixed-sign inputs, exact integers, NaN/inf passthrough
- bit-equal comparison against torch.round on F64 tensors via to_bits()
- skips silently when torch unavailable
- ft-conformance lib tests +1
close_reason: Shipped: 207543d. torch_round_decimals_subprocess_conformance pins 16 cases including half-tie boundaries (torch round-half-to-even), positive/negative decimals, IEEE specials. Tagged-string wire encoding. bit-equal comparison via to_bits().

title: Add fail-loud guards for SVD-class linalg ops with requires_grad
description:
tensor_linalg_svd, svdvals, eigh, eigvalsh, qr, cholesky, and matrix_rank all silently sever autograd by extracting tensor_values, calling kernel-direct factorization, and rebuilding non-grad leaves. With the recent fix to cond and vector_norm, the silent severance pattern is the only remaining linalg autograd hole.

PyTorch supports gradients through SVD/eigh/qr/cholesky via well-known backward formulas, but implementing all of them requires substantial work. The interim fix is to add fail-loud guards on these ops when input requires_grad — matches the pattern used for det/slogdet (pvfk) and stft/istft autograd guards.

This unblocks downstream callers of these ops to fail loud rather than getting silent NaN gradients on their parameters. matrix_rank is integer-valued (intrinsically non-differentiable) so just guard it; the rest can be unguarded once a future bead implements proper backward.

Affects (all in crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs):
- tensor_linalg_svd (12577)
- tensor_linalg_svdvals (12599)
- tensor_linalg_eigh (12547)
- tensor_linalg_eigvalsh (12561)
- tensor_linalg_qr (12972)
- tensor_linalg_cholesky (11918)
- tensor_linalg_matrix_rank (12934)

title: [code-review] roll large shifts overflow before modulo
description: Review finding in recent typed-storage roll path. Repro: construct a length-2 float tensor and call tensor_roll(input, i64::MAX, 0). Current roll_slice computes old_coord + shift before rem_euclid, so debug builds can panic on user input and release builds can wrap instead of PyTorch torch.roll modulo semantics. Expected: no panic; shift is reduced modulo dim and returns [2.0, 1.0].
close_reason: Duplicate roll large-shift review finding; fixed by the same normalized-shift roll patch and conformance probe.

title: perfect-e2e: Conv2d + PixelShuffle super-resolution training test
description: Add an E2E perfect-e2e test for a tiny super-resolution stack: Conv2d (in_ch → out_ch * r^2) → PixelShuffle(r). Trains with Adam against a fixed random target and asserts MSE decreases by at least 2x. Locks the gradient flow through PixelShuffle which has no parameters but reshapes channels into spatial dimensions; commonly used in modern super-resolution models. Currently missing from the perfect-e2e battery.
close_reason: Added conv2d_pixel_shuffle_super_resolution_trains_end_to_end_with_adam — Conv2d(2 → 4, k=3, p=1) → PixelShuffle(2) on 1×2×2×2 input, MSE against 1×1×4×4 zeros. Trains 200 Adam steps; expects 10x loss reduction. Locks gradient flow through PixelShuffle's reshape + permute + reshape composition. ft-conformance now at 146 base lib tests, 190 with --features fuzz.

title: Add E2E training test for functional_conv3d
description:
Add a perfect-e2e regression test that wires a small functional_conv3d into a training loop. Validates the recently fixed 3D conv autograd flow (frankentorch-lgj2) end-to-end through Adam.

Setup:
- 1x1x4x4x4 fixed input (3D image)
- Learnable Conv3d weight (1->1, 2x2x2 kernel)
- Output shape: 1x1x3x3x3
- MSE against target (3x3x3 ones)
- Adam lr=0.05 for 200 steps
- Assert final loss < initial * 0.1

title: ConvTranspose1d bias broadcast uses tensor_expand on wrong rank
description:
ConvTranspose1d::forward (line 7476) calls tensor_expand on bias (shape [1, out_channels]) targeting shape [1, out_channels, 1]. tensor_expand requires the input rank to match the target — it only broadcasts dims of size 1, it doesn't add new dims. So this errors with ShapeMismatch when use_bias=true.

Fix: tensor_reshape the bias from [1, out_channels] to [1, out_channels, 1] first, then tensor_expand to [batch_size, out_channels, l_out].

Affects: crates/ft-nn/src/lib.rs ConvTranspose1d::forward (line 7474+).

title: porting: add tensor_isclose for torch.isclose parity
description: torch.isclose(a, b, rtol=1e-5, atol=1e-8, equal_nan=False) returns an element-wise boolean mask: |a - b| <= atol + rtol * |b|. Returns 0.0/1.0 floats (matching the existing convention for tensor_eq, tensor_lt, etc.). Non-differentiable — fail-loud on requires_grad like other comparison ops. Useful for fine-grained element-wise comparisons, especially in property-based tests.
close_reason: Added tensor_isclose op for torch.isclose parity. Returns 0/1 float mask via |a - b| <= atol + rtol * |b| with PyTorch-matching NaN handling (NaN never close unless equal_nan=true and both are NaN; ±inf close only to same-signed inf). Non-differentiable: fail-loud on requires_grad. 5 unit tests covering close/far pairs, NaN with both equal_nan settings, ±inf handling, requires_grad guard, and negative-tolerance rejection. ft-api now at 1228 lib tests.

title: Add torch.special math functions (expit, logit, erfinv, gammaln, digamma)
description: Implement key torch.special functions: expit (sigmoid), logit, erfinv, gammaln, digamma, polygamma, multigammaln, xlog1py, entr. These are commonly used in probabilistic ML and needed for drop-in parity.
close_reason: Implemented: expit, logit, erfinv, gammaln, digamma, polygamma, multigammaln, xlog1py, entr with 13 tests. All 940 ft-api tests passing.

title: Add torch.polar, cartesian_prod, combinations
description: Quick-win tensor creation ops: polar (magnitude+phase), cartesian_prod, combinations.
close_reason: Implemented tensor_polar, tensor_cartesian_prod, tensor_combinations. 6 tests passing.

title: Implement general N-D tensor_kron instead of 1D/2D placeholder
description: mock-code-finder found a real placeholder in crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs: tensor_kron only handles (1D,1D) and (2D,2D), then returns ShapeMismatch for all other rank combinations with the comment 'For simplicity, only support 1D and 2D for now'. PyTorch torch.kron supports arbitrary-rank tensors after left-padding the lower-rank operand with singleton dimensions, with output shape dim_i = a_dim_i * b_dim_i. Fix tensor_kron to compute general rank-aligned row-major Kronecker products, preserve the existing 1D/2D behavior, fail closed on shape-volume overflow, and add tests covering mixed-rank and 3D cases.
close_reason: Implemented general rank-aligned tensor_kron with mixed-rank and 3D regression tests

title: [testing-fuzzing] add fuzz target for ft-core TensorMeta::from_shape_and_strides + validate
description:
Existing fuzz coverage in fuzz/fuzz_targets/ targets format parsers (state_dict, safetensors, checkpoint, raptorq sidecar, dispatch schema, sparse tensors) but NOT the TensorMeta layout validator at ft-core/src/lib.rs:209.

TensorMeta::from_shape_and_strides + validate() does checked usize arithmetic on caller-supplied (shape, strides, storage_offset) tuples — exactly the surface where stride/size/offset combinations can overflow, alias, or otherwise drive the validator into a panic-on-input path. The validator computes per-axis span via checked_mul(stride, size-1) and accumulates via checked_add, so a regression in either guard would surface as a release-build panic on user-supplied tensor layouts.

Concrete acceptance:
- new fuzz target ft_core_tensor_meta drives from_shape_and_strides with arbitrary shape/strides/offset/dtype (decoded from raw fuzz bytes, capped to 8-rank tensors and small dimension sizes to keep the fuzzer iterating)
- on success, exercises shape() / strides() / numel() / is_contiguous() — these are the post-validate accessors callers reach for, and any post-validate panic indicates a missing pre-condition check
- includes at least 1 corpus seed
- registered in fuzz/Cargo.toml [[bin]] section
- compiles cleanly under rch exec -- cargo check --manifest-path fuzz/Cargo.toml
close_reason: Shipped: 42358fc. New fuzz target ft_core_tensor_meta drives TensorMeta::from_shape_and_strides + validate with arbitrary shape/stride/offset/dtype tuples, exercises every post-validate accessor (numel/is_contiguous/shape/strides/storage_offset/dtype/device), and includes 3 corpus seeds. Compiles cleanly via cargo check on rch worker.

title: Metamorphic: pixel_unshuffle(pixel_shuffle(x, r), r) == x bit-exact
description: The existing pixel_shuffle_unshuffle_roundtrip test in ft-api only checks SHAPES are preserved, not VALUES. Both ops are pure tensor reshape + permute (no arithmetic), so the round-trip should be bit-exactly identity. Add a fuzz_metamorphic property test in ft-conformance that verifies bit-exact value preservation across random 4-D inputs. Catches any silent permutation-axis bug in either op.
close_reason: Added fuzz_metamorphic_pixel_shuffle_unshuffle_round_trip — proptest with random 4-D inputs (n,c,h,w bounded; r ∈ {1,2,3}), entries bounded by ±2048/23. Asserts pixel_unshuffle(pixel_shuffle(x, r), r) is BIT-EXACTLY identical to x (since both ops are pure reshape + permute with no arithmetic). The pre-existing pixel_shuffle_unshuffle_roundtrip test in ft-api only checked SHAPES; this fills in the value-preservation invariant. ft-conformance now at 192 fuzz tests.

title: tensor_diagonal is severed-autograd: linear backward (inverse of diag_embed)
description:
tensor_diagonal at lib.rs:8232 extracts the diagonal of a 2-D matrix (with optional offset). The forward is purely linear in the input: output[i] = input[row_start + i, col_start + i]. PyTorch's torch.diagonal is differentiable.

Backward (the inverse of diag_embed):
   grad_input[row_start + i, col_start + i] = grad_output[i]
   grad_input is zero elsewhere.

Wrap the F64 path in tensor_apply_function with this analytical backward. F32 path keeps the existing fail-loud behavior to preserve dtype semantics (consistent with the affine_grid / linalg_inv pattern). Surfaced during /reality-check pass.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Wrapped the F64 path of tensor_diagonal in tensor_apply_function with the inverse-of-diag_embed backward: grad_input[row_start + i, col_start + i] = grad_output[i], all other positions 0. F32 path adds an explicit fail-loud guard for requires_grad=true (consistent with affine_grid / linalg_inv pattern; tensor_apply_function is f64-only). Captured (m, n, row_start, col_start, diag_len) via closure. Added diagonal_propagates_gradient_via_scatter_to_diag_positions regression test (3x3 main diagonal under sum loss → grad = identity matrix, asserts to 1e-12). ft-api lib tests: 1149.

title: Add fail-loud guard on one_hot for requires_grad input
description:
tensor_one_hot extracts indices, builds binary mask, and rebuilds non-grad leaf. Silent severance through requires_grad input. Add fail-loud guard — input is intrinsically non-differentiable (class indices) but the silent severance is unhelpful for users.

Affects: crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs one_hot (line 11679).

title: Implement tensor_triangular_solve autograd [child of tw0y]
description:
Wrap tensor_triangular_solve in tensor_apply_function with the analytical backward.
For X = A^{-1} B (A triangular):
   grad_B = A^{-T} grad_X = triangular_solve(A^T, grad_X, !upper)
   grad_A = -A^{-T} grad_X X^T   masked to triangular pattern of A
The grad_A computation can either reuse triangular_solve(A^T, grad_X, !upper) for the leading factor and then outer-product with X^T, or compute the n×n result directly. Either way, save A and X for backward.

Note: detach A's gradient from non-triangular positions — PyTorch returns 0 in those positions. The mask (upper or lower triangle) must match the input convention.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Wrapped tensor_triangular_solve in tensor_apply_function with the analytical backward grad_B = A^{-T} grad_X (via inline triangular_solve on A^T with !upper) and grad_A = -A^{-T} grad_X X^T masked to A's triangle. A and X are saved for backward. Added triangular_solve_propagates_gradient_through_a_and_b regression test (diagonal A, lower triangle, sum loss → analytical [[-2.5, 0], [-1.25, -1.25]] for grad_A, [0.5, 0.25] for grad_B). ft-api lib tests: 1141.

title: Add f32 support to nonzero/count_nonzero/diff
close_reason: Completed

title: Add fail-loud guards on LU decomposition primitives
description:
tensor_lu_factor, tensor_linalg_lu, tensor_lu_solve in ft-api silently sever autograd by extracting tensor_values, running LU/solve in plain f64, and rebuilding non-grad leaves. Match the pattern used for SVD/eigh/QR/Cholesky (frankentorch-rl4g): add fail-loud guards on requires_grad input.

LU backward is implementable but non-trivial (involves a triangular system per factor). For now, surface the silent severance so users get loud errors instead of silent NaN gradients.

Affects (all in crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs):
- tensor_lu_factor (line 11828)
- tensor_linalg_lu (line 11846)
- tensor_lu_solve (line 11868)

title: Native state dict serialization rejects F16/BF16 tensors
description: testing-conformance-harnesses found a serialization contract gap in crates/ft-serialize/src/lib.rs. Native FrankenTorch state dict save/load only maps DType::F64 and DType::F32 to dtype tags, while ft-core DenseTensor and SafeTensors serialization already support DType::F16 and DType::BF16. This means half-precision tensors cannot round-trip through save_state_dict/load_state_dict even though the tensor stack supports them. Add stable native dtype tags for F16/BF16, encode/decode their 2-byte little-endian payloads, update the old unsupported-dtype test, and add round-trip coverage for F16 and BF16.
close_reason: Completed: native state dict serialization now round-trips F16/BF16 tensors with stable dtype tags and byte-exact tests.

title: Run perf-SLO measurement against new 100x baseline
description:
Follow-up to frankentorch-olf.

The canonical perf baseline is now a 100-iteration full-profile bundle at artifacts/phase2c/performance/perf_slo_baseline_v1.json.

Next work:
1. Capture a fresh perf measurement using --baseline artifacts/phase2c/performance/perf_slo_baseline_v1.json.
2. Re-assess the training_step_memory, backward_heavy_allocation, and benchmark_family_tail regression budgets now that a stable baseline exists.
3. Decide whether the baseline should be refreshed again after any harness or kernel changes.
close_reason: Captured fresh perf-SLO measurement against 100x baseline. Results: 4/8 budgets pass (elementwise_forward, memory_footprint, allocation_churn, tail_stability). Regression budgets all pass with baseline comparison. Remaining failures are matmul_core (expected for pure Rust), backward_pass, optimizer_step, checkpoint_roundtrip.

title: Refresh FEATURE_PARITY ledger after parity closure
close_reason: Completed

title: tensor_logsumexp is severed-autograd: dim-reducing softmax-weighted backward
description:
tensor_logsumexp at lib.rs:14728 computes y[outer, inner] = max + log(sum_d exp(x[outer, d, inner] - max)) but rebuilds the output as a requires_grad=false leaf. PyTorch's torch.logsumexp is differentiable.

Backward identity:
   y = log(sum_j exp(x_j))
   dy/dx_i = exp(x_i) / sum_j exp(x_j) = exp(x_i - y)
For the dim-reducing form:
   grad_x[outer, d, inner] = grad_y[outer, inner] * exp(x[outer, d, inner] - y[outer, inner])

The exp(x - y) factor is bounded in [0, 1] (since y >= max(x) in the max-subtract form), so the backward is numerically stable without needing a separate normalizer.

Wrap in tensor_apply_function with this analytical backward. Save x and y for backward — y is needed to construct the softmax weights without recomputing logsumexp. Surfaced during /reality-check pass.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Wrapped tensor_logsumexp in tensor_apply_function with the analytical backward grad_x_d = grad_y * exp(x_d - y). The exp(x - y) factor is in [0, 1] (since y >= max(x) under max-subtract), so the backward is numerically stable without a separate normalizer. Both x and y are saved for backward; the forward already computed y so it's free to save. Added logsumexp_propagates_gradient_via_softmax_weights regression test (x=[1,2,3] under sum loss → grad = softmax(x) ≈ [0.0900, 0.2447, 0.6652], with sanity sum-to-1 check). ft-api lib tests: 1148.

title: Add f32 support to tensor_diagonal and searchsorted
close_reason: Completed

title: Document trait default method contracts with doc comments
description:
## Context
The stub/placeholder audit (2026-04-02) found 8 trait default methods that return trivial values. All are architecturally correct but several lack doc comments explaining WHY the default is what it is and WHEN implementors should override.

Prerequisite beads completed:
- frankentorch-jhi (CLOSED): Changed Optimizer::set_momentum to return Result
- frankentorch-tk5 (CLOSED): Added module_type to ModuleRegistrationError::Unsupported, using std::any::type_name::<Self>() in the trait default

## Affected methods (8 total)

### Module trait (ft-nn/src/lib.rs)
Current state (verified 2026-04-03):

1. `named_parameters_own()` -> Vec::new()
   - Has doc: "Return this module's own named parameters (non-recursive)." + explanation about containers
   - NEEDS: "# Default behavior" and "# When to override" sections

2. `named_parameter_slots_own()` -> Vec::new()
   - Has doc: "Return dynamically-registered own parameters, including optional slots."
   - NEEDS: default/override guidance

3. `named_buffer_slots_own()` -> Vec::new()
   - Has doc: "Return own registered buffers, including persistence metadata."
   - NEEDS: default/override guidance

4. `named_children()` -> Vec::new()
   - Has doc: "Return direct child sub-modules with their local names." + naming conventions
   - NEEDS: explicit note that leaf modules should keep the default

5. `register_parameter()` -> Err(Unsupported{module_type, operation})
   - Has doc: "Register (or replace) a named parameter slot."
   - NEEDS: note about default returning Unsupported with module_type context, and when to override

6. `register_buffer()` -> Err(Unsupported{module_type, operation})
   - Has doc: "Register (or replace) a named buffer slot."
   - NEEDS: same as register_parameter

### Optimizer trait (ft-optim/src/lib.rs)
Current state (verified 2026-04-03):

7. `get_momentum()` -> None
   - Has doc: "Return momentum if the optimizer exposes one."
   - NEEDS: note that None means the optimizer has no momentum concept, and override when momentum exists (e.g., SGD)

8. `set_momentum()` -> Err(unsupported)
   - Has doc: "Set momentum for optimizers that expose it."
   - NEEDS: note about the Result return, when to override, and that non-momentum optimizers return Err

## Doc comment enhancement template
For EACH method, ENHANCE (do not replace) the existing doc comment by appending:

```rust
///
/// # Default behavior
/// Returns [specific value]. This is correct for [category of types] because [reason].
///
/// # When to override
/// Override this when [condition]. Example: [`Linear`] overrides this to [what it does].
```

Do NOT delete or modify existing doc comment text — only append the new sections.

## Plan
1. Read ft-nn/src/lib.rs and ft-optim/src/lib.rs to find exact current line numbers
2. For each of the 8 methods, append the default/override sections to the existing doc comment
3. Verify: `rch exec -- cargo doc -p ft-nn -p ft-optim --no-deps 2>&1` — zero warnings
4. Verify: `rch exec -- cargo clippy -p ft-nn -p ft-optim -- -D warnings` — clean
5. Verify: `git diff` shows ONLY doc comment changes, zero logic modifications

## Testing requirements

### Automated verification
- `rch exec -- cargo doc -p ft-nn -p ft-optim --no-deps` — zero warnings
- `rch exec -- cargo test -p ft-nn -p ft-optim` — all existing tests pass (doc changes can't break tests, but verify)
- `rch exec -- cargo clippy -p ft-nn -p ft-optim --all-targets -- -D warnings` — clean

### Manual verification (implementing agent must confirm)
- [ ] All 8 methods have "# Default behavior" section
- [ ] All 8 methods have "# When to override" section
- [ ] No method signature or logic was changed
- [ ] Existing doc text was preserved (only appended to)

## Acceptance Criteria
- All 8 methods have enhanced doc comments with default behavior + override guidance
- cargo doc clean
- cargo test clean
- cargo clippy clean
- git diff shows only comment additions

## Dependencies
- frankentorch-tk5 (CLOSED) — added module_type to Unsupported variant, docs must reflect this
- frankentorch-jhi (CLOSED) — changed set_momentum signature, docs must reflect Result return

## Reasoning
Future auditors (human or AI) encountering Vec::new() defaults may incorrectly flag them as stubs. Explicit "# Default behavior" sections prevent this and serve as self-documenting contracts.
close_reason: Expanded default-trait docs for ft-nn Module traversal/registration hooks and ft-optim momentum hooks, then verified cargo doc/check/clippy via rch

title: porting: add tensor_softshrink + tensor_hardshrink for torch parity
description:
Two missing activations:
  - softshrink(x, lambd): x - lambd if x > lambd, x + lambd if x < -lambd, else 0. The proximal operator of L1 norm; used in ISTA/FISTA/sparse coding.
  - hardshrink(x, lambd): x if |x| > lambd, else 0.
Both compose via tensor_gt/lt + tensor_where; autograd-aware. Derivative is 1 outside [-lambd, lambd] and 0 inside (the where masks gradient correctly).
close_reason: Added tensor_softshrink and tensor_hardshrink in ft-api with parameterized lambd. softshrink: composes via tensor_gt + tensor_lt + chained tensor_where + tensor_sub/add (mutually exclusive masks for the two outside regions). hardshrink: tensor_abs + tensor_gt + tensor_where. Both fail-loud on negative lambd. Autograd-correct: gradient is 1 outside [-lambd, lambd] and 0 inside (the where routes the inside region to no-grad zeros). 5 unit tests cover known values, gradient regression, boundary handling, and negative-lambd rejection. ft-api now at 1307 lib tests.

title: Implement SparseAdam optimizer
description: Add SparseAdam optimizer to ft-optim. Adam variant for sparse gradients. Critical for NLP models with sparse embeddings.
close_reason: Implemented SparseAdam optimizer with sparse-only moment updates, bias correction, validation. 4 tests passing.

title: [deadlock-finder][performance] wait_for_legacy_oracle_exit busy-yields the scheduler instead of sleeping
description:
ft-conformance/src/lib.rs:9233 calls std::thread::yield_now() inside the legacy-oracle wait loop. yield_now() hints the scheduler to release the CPU but immediately reschedules — under no-contention or high-priority scheduling the loop tightens into a near-spin that polls child.try_wait() and Instant::now() at microsecond cadence.

The downstream cost: every legacy-oracle subprocess invocation pegs the CPU at ~100% on at least one core for the duration of the child's runtime. With dozens of subprocess oracles in the conformance suite (torch_*_subprocess_conformance, scipy diff tests, raptorq pipeline checks) this multiplies the wall-clock CPU usage of the suite without buying anything — the child takes milliseconds to react to either overflow-kill or natural exit, so a 1-2 ms sleep cadence is plenty.

Concrete fix: replace yield_now() with std::thread::sleep(Duration::from_millis(2)). Polling stays well under the millisecond timeout-budget granularity (the existing test wait_for_legacy_oracle_exit_times_out_fail_closed asserts exit within seconds, not ms). Overflow-detection latency increases by at most 2 ms — negligible vs the already-observed 30-second timeout budget on the legacy oracle.

Acceptance:
- yield_now() replaced with sleep(2ms)
- existing wait_for_legacy_oracle_exit_times_out_fail_closed test passes
- ft-conformance lib tests count unchanged
- compiles cleanly under rch exec
close_reason: Shipped: d8dcd15. yield_now replaced with sleep(2ms) in wait_for_legacy_oracle_exit. Polling cadence well under timeout-budget granularity; overflow-detection latency grows by at most 2 ms. Existing timeout test passes.

title: Pre-existing test failure: custom_function_inside_no_grad_does_not_record_on_tape
description:
Pre-existing failure from commit ec29fb5 (feat(autograd,conformance): add custom function grad-disable tests). Test asserts s.tensor_grad_fn(y).unwrap().is_none() but the assertion fails. This is unrelated to gradient checkpointing or mixed precision work — verified by stashing recent changes and reproducing the failure.

Test location: crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs:26666

The test creates a tensor_apply_function inside no_grad context and expects:
1. tensor_requires_grad(y) returns false ✓ (this part passes)
2. tensor_grad_fn(y) returns None ✗ (this part fails)
3. tensor_backward(y) returns TensorRootDoesNotRequireGrad

The fix likely requires ensuring tensor_apply_function records the grad_fn as None when grad is disabled in the parent session.
close_reason: Fixed: TensorTape::apply_function now records the output as a Leaf node (not CustomFunction) when grad is disabled or no input requires grad. This matches PyTorch behavior where ops created inside torch.no_grad() produce non-gradient tensors. The pre-existing test now passes, all 8 custom_function tests pass, all 309 ft-autograd tests pass, and gradient checkpointing still works correctly.

title: tensor_cdist severs autograd; broadcastable composition for finite p > 0
description:
tensor_cdist at lib.rs:4230 computes pairwise L_p distances between two sets of vectors. For finite p > 0 the math is composable through autograd-aware primitives via broadcasted subtraction.

Strategy for shape [P, M] x [R, M]:
  x1_b = unsqueeze(x1, 1)    → [P, 1, M]
  x2_b = unsqueeze(x2, 0)    → [1, R, M]
  diff = x1_b - x2_b         → [P, R, M] (broadcast)
  d    = (sum_k |diff[k]|^p)^(1/p)

For batched [B, P, M] x [B, R, M] the same pattern works at dims 2 and 1.

Sister of frankentorch-0i8u (pdist). p == 0 / p == +inf still fail-loud on requires_grad.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Composed tensor_cdist through unsqueeze + expand + sub + abs + pow + sum_dim + pow primitives for both 2-D and 3-D batched inputs. The broadcasted subtraction handles both [P, M] x [R, M] and [B, P, M] x [B, R, M] cases. p == 0 / p == +inf still fail-loud on requires_grad to make autograd severance explicit (deferred to a follow-up — same pattern as pdist 0i8u). Added cdist_l2_propagates_gradient_through_both_inputs regression test (single-pair x1=[[0,0]], x2=[[3,4]], p=2 → grad_x1=[-3/5,-4/5], grad_x2=[3/5,4/5]). ft-api lib tests: 1170.

title: Add complex number ops: real, imag, conj, complex, view_as_real, view_as_complex
close_reason: Completed

title: Enrich Module register_parameter/register_buffer errors with module type context
description:
## Context
The stub/placeholder audit (2026-04-02) found that Module::register_parameter() and Module::register_buffer() default implementations (ft-nn/src/lib.rs) return `Err(ModuleRegistrationError::Unsupported { operation: "register_parameter" })`.

The error tells you WHAT operation failed but not WHICH module rejected it. When debugging a deep Sequential model with 50+ layers, "register_parameter unsupported" gives no clue about which layer is the problem.

## Current error type
```rust
pub enum ModuleRegistrationError {
    InvalidName { kind: &'static str },
    NameConflict { name: String },
    Unsupported { operation: &'static str },
}
```

## Design decision: How to get the module type name in a trait default

The trait default method signature is `fn register_parameter(&mut self, ...)`. We CANNOT call `std::any::type_name::<Self>()` because the default method has `Self: ?Sized` (it's on `dyn Module`).

**Solution**: Add a new required method to the Module trait:
```rust
fn module_type_name(&self) -> &'static str;
```
This is cheap (one &'static str per impl), gives correct type info even through dyn dispatch, and follows the same pattern PyTorch uses with `_get_name()`.

Each module implementation returns its name: "Linear", "Conv2d", "Sequential", etc.

The default for register_parameter/register_buffer then becomes:
```rust
fn register_parameter(...) -> Result<(), ModuleRegistrationError> {
    Err(ModuleRegistrationError::Unsupported {
        operation: "register_parameter",
        module_type: self.module_type_name(),
    })
}
```

## Plan
1. Add `module_type_name(&self) -> &'static str` to the Module trait (no default — every impl must provide it)
2. Add `module_type: &'static str` field to `ModuleRegistrationError::Unsupported`
3. Update the Display impl to print "{module_type} does not support {operation}"
4. Update default register_parameter/register_buffer to include module_type
5. Add `module_type_name()` to ALL existing Module implementations (~40+ structs):
   - Linear -> "Linear", Conv1d -> "Conv1d", Sequential -> "Sequential", etc.
6. Update any tests that construct or match ModuleRegistrationError::Unsupported

## Testing requirements

### Unit tests (in ft-nn #[cfg(test)])

#### test_unsupported_register_includes_module_type
- Call register_parameter on a module that doesn't override it (e.g., ReLU)
- Assert the error message contains "ReLU"
- Assert the error message contains "register_parameter"

#### test_unsupported_register_buffer_includes_module_type
- Same for register_buffer on a leaf module

#### test_all_modules_have_type_name
- For each major module type (Linear, Conv1d, Conv2d, LSTM, Sequential, etc.):
  - Construct an instance
  - Assert module_type_name() returns a non-empty string
  - Assert module_type_name() does NOT contain "Module" alone (should be specific)

#### test_module_type_name_matches_struct_name
- For a selection of modules, verify that module_type_name() matches the Rust struct name
- E.g., Linear::module_type_name() == "Linear"

#### test_sequential_register_parameter_succeeds
- Verify that Sequential (which DOES support register_parameter) still works correctly
- Construct a Sequential, register a parameter, verify it appears in named_parameter_slots_own()

### E2E validation
- Run `rch exec -- cargo test -p ft-nn` — all existing + new tests pass
- Run `rch exec -- cargo clippy -p ft-nn --all-targets -- -D warnings` — clean

## Acceptance Criteria
- Error messages include the module type name (e.g., "ReLU does not support register_parameter")
- ALL Module implementations have module_type_name()
- 5+ new tests covering the enriched error paths
- Existing tests still pass
- Clippy clean

## Risk
Medium — this adds a required trait method to ~40 implementations. Use grep to find all `impl Module for` blocks. Each just needs one line added. Can be parallelized with subagents.

## Dependencies
None — isolated change within ft-nn.
close_reason: ModuleRegistrationError::Unsupported now carries module_type context, default Module registration methods report std::any::type_name::<Self>(), and ft-nn test plus workspace check/clippy passed via rch

title: tensor_interpolate bicubic mode autograd via 16-corner cubic Hermite gather
description:
After frankentorch-3t1t added linear/bilinear/trilinear autograd, bicubic still fails loud. PyTorch's bicubic interpolation uses cubic Hermite spline weights for 4 source pixels per axis (16 total in 2-D). Compose via 16 tensor_index_select calls + non-grad weight tensors + accumulating tensor_add — same partition-of-unity weighted blend as bilinear, just with 16 corners and cubic-Hermite weights.

Per-axis cubic Hermite weights (PyTorch's bicubic_convolution variant, A=-0.75):
  w0 = A*t*(t-1)^2
  w1 = (A+2)*t^3 - (A+3)*t^2 + 1
  w2 = -(A+2)*t^3 + (2A+3)*t^2 - A*t
  w3 = -A*t^2*(t-1)
where t = frac(src). Source pixels are at floor(src) - 1, floor(src), floor(src)+1, floor(src)+2 (clamped).

Affects: crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs tensor_interpolate (line 14674).

title: Add logsumexp, dist, movedim, aminmax, unfold, renorm, isin
close_reason: Completed

title: pack_padded_sequence and pad_packed_sequence sever autograd
description:
Both pack_padded_sequence and pad_packed_sequence in ft-nn extract tensor_values from input, manually permute/copy to a new layout, and rebuild via session.tensor_variable(_, _, false). This silently severs autograd through every RNN/LSTM/GRU training pipeline that uses torch.nn.utils.rnn.pack_padded_sequence or pad_packed_sequence (which is most of them).

Fix:
- pack_padded_sequence: reshape input to [T*B, F], compute the flat index list (for each timestep t with batch_size bs[t], emit t*B + sorted_indices[s] for s in 0..bs[t]), then call tensor_index_select to gather the rows. Shape back if needed.
- pad_packed_sequence: invert the operation. The packed data has shape [total_elements/F, F]; build a [B*T, F] output by scatter-then-pad. Where each packed row k corresponds to (t, sorted_indices[s]), set output row at orig_batch * T + t. Use tensor_scatter (autograd-aware) and a non-grad fill tensor for padding_value.

Affects: crates/ft-nn/src/lib.rs pack_padded_sequence (line 13364), pad_packed_sequence (line 13490).

title: soft_margin_loss severs autograd; composable through neg/mul/relu/abs/log1p/exp
description:
soft_margin_loss at lib.rs:10423 computes log(1 + exp(-t*x)) per element in plain f64, builds the per-element loss as a requires_grad=false leaf, then takes tensor_mean — but the gradient can no longer reach input or target because the leaf has severed the tape.

Math: with z = -t*x,
   log1p(exp(z)) = max(0, z) + log1p(exp(-|z|))
                 = relu(z) + log1p(exp(-abs(z)))

Composable through autograd-aware primitives:
  z = tensor_mul(tensor_neg(target), input)
  loss_per_elem = tensor_add(tensor_relu(z), tensor_log1p(tensor_exp(tensor_neg(tensor_abs(z)))))
  loss = tensor_mean(loss_per_elem)

Same numerical-stability form, same per-element math, but with the autograd tape preserved.

Sister of frankentorch-ilwr / 4liu (other severed loss functions). Surfaced during /reality-check pass.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Composed soft_margin_loss through neg/mul/relu/abs/log1p/exp/add/mean primitives — same numerical-stability form (relu(z) + log1p(exp(-|z|)) where z = -t*x) but with the autograd tape preserved. Added soft_margin_loss_propagates_gradient_through_input regression test (input=[1,-1], target=[1,-1] under mean loss → analytical [-1/(2(1+e)), 1/(2(1+e))]). ft-api lib tests: 1153.

title: Severed-autograd in linalg ops: cholesky_solve, linalg_inv, triangular_solve, slogdet, det
description:
After closing matrix_norm (frankentorch-i4rd), several other linalg ops in ft-api still extract values, run a kernel, and rebuild a requires_grad=false leaf — same severed-autograd pattern fixed under frankentorch-1tax for cov / corrcoef / mode etc.

Affected ops:
  - tensor_linalg_inv (lib.rs:11485) — d(A^{-1})/dA: grad_A = -A^{-T} grad_out A^{-T}. Composable via matmul + transpose + neg, with A^{-1} saved.
  - tensor_triangular_solve (lib.rs:11500) — d X / dB = A^{-T}, dX/dA contracted with grad gives an outer-product term masked to the triangular pattern.
  - tensor_cholesky_solve (lib.rs:11453) — chains through cholesky factor; gradient flows through the factor's triangular_solve calls.
  - tensor_linalg_det / tensor_linalg_slogdet — Jacobi formula: d log|det A| / dA = A^{-T}; det case: d det A / dA = det(A) * A^{-T}.

PyTorch's torch.linalg.{inv,solve_triangular,cholesky_solve,det,slogdet} are all differentiable. Surfaced during a /reality-check pass scanning for  and  patterns in linalg op bodies.

Each op gets its own child bead. Priority order based on usage frequency: linalg_inv → triangular_solve → cholesky_solve → det/slogdet.
notes:
All TensorNodeId-returning linalg ops now have autograd:
  - tensor_linalg_inv         frq7 (d364f8e)  — Y^T grad Y^T backward
  - tensor_triangular_solve   skpl (163365f)  — substitution backward
  - tensor_cholesky_solve     this commit     — composes through triangular_solve
  
The det / slogdet ops were originally listed as children but they currently return raw f64 (not TensorNodeId), so they're outside the autograd graph entirely. Adding autograd to them requires an API change (return TensorNodeId), which is a separate concern from the severed-autograd fix this umbrella tracked. Filed as a separate concern if needed.

ft-api lib tests: 1142 passing.

title: Add E2E training test for BatchNorm1d + Linear regressor
description:
Add a perfect-e2e training test that exercises BatchNorm1d's training-mode running-statistics path + affine parameters + a Linear head. Validates that the autograd composition through tensor_mean_dim, tensor_sub, tensor_mul, tensor_div, tensor_sqrt actually produces gradients that converge under Adam.

Setup:
- Input batch [4, 3] (4 samples, 3 features)
- BatchNorm1d(3) (with affine weight + bias, running stats)
- Linear(3 -> 1)
- MSE loss against [4, 1] target
- Adam lr=0.05 for 200 steps
- Assert final loss < initial * 0.1

Actual members include ft-nn, ft-optim, and ft-data, and ft-conformance/ft-runtime responsibilities are broader than the README implies. Update public architecture docs to match the real layering and crate purposes discovered during codebase archaeology.
